examples.title: Selection Operation in Relational Algebra
examples.description: Demonstrates how to select tuples from Chapters relation based on a condition
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Selection(Relation R, Condition C)
BEGIN
  Result ← empty set
  FOR EACH tuple t IN R DO
    IF t satisfies condition C THEN
      Add t to Result
    END IF
  END FOR
  RETURN Result
END

// Example: σ(Pages > 30)(Chapters)
// Returns all chapters with more than 30 pages
examples.explanation: Selection (σ) is a unary operation that extracts rows (tuples) from a relation that satisfy a specified condition. It acts as a horizontal filter, keeping only qualifying tuples.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of tuples in relation
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is number of qualifying tuples

examples.title: Projection Operation in Relational Algebra
examples.description: Demonstrates how to select specific columns from Chapters relation
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Projection(Relation R, AttributeList L)
BEGIN
  Result ← empty set
  FOR EACH tuple t IN R DO
    newTuple ← extract attributes L from t
    Add newTuple to Result
  END FOR
  // Remove duplicate tuples if required
  Result ← DISTINCT(Result)
  RETURN Result
END

// Example: π(ChapterTitle, Pages)(Chapters)
// Returns only chapter titles and page counts
examples.explanation: Projection (π) is a unary operation that selects specific columns (attributes) from a relation, creating a vertical subset. It removes duplicate tuples from the result.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of tuples

examples.title: Natural Join Operation
examples.description: Demonstrates join operation to combine Chapters and Books tables based on BookID
examples.explanation: Natural Join combines two relations based on common attribute names (BookID in this case). Each chapter is paired with its corresponding book information where BookID matches.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × m) where n and m are sizes of input relations
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n × m) in worst case for result

examples.title: SQL Implementation of Relational Operations
examples.description: Demonstrates Selection, Projection, and Join using SQL on Chapters and Books tables
examples.code:
-- Create Chapters table with constraints
CREATE TABLE Chapters (
    ChapterID INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    ChapterTitle VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    Pages INTEGER,
    BookID INTEGER,
    FOREIGN KEY (BookID) REFERENCES Books(BookID)
);

-- Selection: Get all chapters with more than 30 pages
SELECT * 
FROM Chapters 
WHERE Pages > 30;

-- Projection: Get only chapter titles and page counts
SELECT ChapterTitle, Pages 
FROM Chapters;

-- Join: Get chapters with their book information
SELECT C.ChapterID, C.ChapterTitle, C.Pages, B.Title AS BookTitle, B.Author
FROM Chapters C
INNER JOIN Books B ON C.BookID = B.BookID;

-- Union: Get all chapter IDs and book IDs (union-compatible)
SELECT ChapterID FROM Chapters
UNION
SELECT BookID FROM Books;
examples.explanation: SQL implements relational algebra operations: SELECT implements σ (selection/project), FROM defines the relation, WHERE implements conditions, and JOIN implements ⋈ operation. The code shows practical SQL equivalents of theoretical relational algebra.
